clusters.title: Teen killed in rollover after attempted carjacking: police
clusters.neutral_headline: Teen killed in rollover after attempted carjacking: police
bullet_summary: Reported by 9News, The Age, ABC Just In

items.title: Teen killed in rollover after attempted carjacking: police
items.standfirst: A teenage boy has been killed when the allegedly stolen car he was in rolled after a collision in southwest Melbourne overnight.
items.content: A teenage boy has been killed when the allegedly stolen car he was in rolled after a collision in south-west Melbourne overnight.The stolen grey Skoda was pursuing another vehicle on Derrimut Road in Hoppers Crossing just after midnight, police said.The Skoda crashed into the back of a Toyota sedan, which police said "managed to safely flee the scene".READ MORE: Two teens killed in e-bike collision with Harley Davidson in BrisbaneShortly afterwards the Skoda crashed into a Ford sedan and Ford Ranger, causing it to roll several times.A teenage boy in the car died in the crash.A 17-year-old boy and 16-year-old girl from the Wyndham area were taken to hospital under police guard with non-life threatening injuries.A 16-year-old girl with minor injuries was also taken to hospital but has since been released. She will be interviewed by police later this morning.The occupants in the Toyota and both Fords suffered minor injuries.The Skoda had been stolen from Maidstonebetween March 11 and 12.Police are asking witnesses or anyone with dashcam or CCTV footage to contact Crime Stoppers.The crash is now being investigated by the Major Collision Investigation Unit.Derrimut Road between Hogans and Heaths roads remain closed to traffic.READ MORE: This is one call Trump can't chicken out ofNEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Two teenagers die in e-bike crash south of Brisbane
clusters.neutral_headline: Two teenagers die in e-bike crash south of Brisbane

items.title: Two teenagers die in e-bike crash south of Brisbane
items.standfirst: Emergency services respond to a crash between an e-bike and motorcycle at Greenbank in Logan.
items.content: Emergency services respond to a crash between an e-bike and motorcycle at Greenbank in Logan.

items.title: Two teens killed in e-bike collision with Harley Davidson in Brisbane
items.standfirst: Two teenagers on an e-bike have died in a collision with a Harley Davidson motorcycle in Brisbane's south.
items.content: Two teenagers on an e-bike have died in a collision with a Harley Davidson motorcycle in Brisbane's south.Police believe the motorcycle collided with the e-bike when it overtook a vehicle on Middle Road in Greenbank about 9pm last night.The boy and girl on the e-bike, both in their teens, died at the scene and the motorcyclist was taken to Princess Alexandra Hospital in a stable condition.LIVE UPDATES: Iran reveals underground 'missile tunnels'Investigating officers don't think the e-bike had lights on at the time of the crash. Police called on anyone who saw the crash or had footage or other information to come forward.The forensic crash unit is investigating.Calls for restrictions on e-bikes have been growing as the number of riders, particularly children, dying or suffering serious injuries grows. Laws vary significantly from state to state and there's no nationally consistent age limit or other regulations.READ MORE: This is one call Trump can't chicken out ofOnly Western Australia imposes an age-limit on e-bike riders .Queensland allows people of any age to ride one on most roads and paths without a licence so long as it complies with power limits. The government is considering the recommendations of a parliamentary inquiry report handed down earlier this month.It included calls for the devices to be restricted to people aged 16 and over and limit footpath speeds to 10km/h, among other changes.NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: Is it possible to take money out of politics?
clusters.neutral_headline: Is it possible to take money out of politics

items.title: Is it possible to take money out of politics?
items.standfirst: Through political donations, moneyed interests are able to access the political process in a way the average Australian simply can not. In an attempt to take money out of politics, South Australian Premier Peter Malinauskas has banned most political donations in the state and capped campaign spending by third parties. Party campaigning is now publicly funded, but the funding is tied to how many votes a party gets, which critics say makes it hard for minor parties or new independents. Now a state election will test if it makes politics fairer.
items.content: Through political donations, moneyed interests are able to access the political process in a way the average Australian simply can not. In an attempt to take money out of politics, South Australian Premier Peter Malinauskas has banned most political donations in the state and capped campaign spending by third parties. Party campaigning is now publicly funded, but the funding is tied to how many votes a party gets, which critics say makes it hard for minor parties or new independents. Now a state election will test if it makes politics fairer.
